description: Intent-first ICP scoring for Air. Rules are split into four categories — intent (behavior), fit (firmographic), persona (who the person is), expansion (whitespace + growth). We weight intent signals higher than firmographic because Air is a PLG-adjacent creative tool: buyers validate themselves by using the product before a seller ever engages. Total scores saturate around 100; tier thresholds match the dashboard so the Python pipeline and the UI never disagree on a lead's tier.

philosophy.reasoning: For a creative-ops tool with a self-serve surface, a prospect's usage tells us more than their org chart. Firmographic signals are necessary (we need to know they can afford us) but rarely sufficient — enterprise leads with zero product usage are common and usually cold. We therefore give the single largest weight to signup intent, and compound it with credit/asset usage rather than stack it additively.

rules.description: 20+ pricing/demo page views. Repeated pricing visits are the single strongest pre-contact intent signal — a prospect checking price multiple times has an internal decision in motion.
10-19 pricing page views — decision-gathering phase.
3-9 pricing page views — research phase, not yet decision.
Source is Product Signup. A prospect who gave us their email in exchange for a workspace has self-qualified further than any form-fill can measure.

Product signup AND >=50% credit usage. A signup alone is cheap; credit usage proves the prospect actually tried the product. This rule stacks on top of intent_signup_product because a *validated* signup is categorically stronger than a dormant one.

Referral source from a business email (not gmail/yahoo). Warm intros outperform product-signups on close rate when the referrer is a current customer at a real company — a referral from a personal email is unverifiable and doesn't get the same lift.
Event-sourced lead. Moderate intent — they showed up in person, but event lists are noisy.
80%+ credit usage. Near-exhaustion of free credits is a classic PQL (product-qualified lead) signal — they're about to hit a wall and will convert if we reach out this week.
50-79% credit usage. Actively trialing — good nurture candidate.
200+ asset uploads. Heavy upload volume means the prospect has migrated real work into our workspace — they've made a switching-cost investment we can lean on.
50-199 asset uploads — real but not yet committed usage.

10+ teammate invites. In a collaboration product this is the viral/expansion signal — the primary user has recruited their team, which means they've already sold the product internally and we just need to close the contract.
5-9 teammate invites — early team adoption.
Enterprise firmographic (1000+ employees). Large budget, long sales cycle, higher ACV. Never fire this on top of low activation — see expansion_activated_enterprise for when size + usage compound.
Mid-market (201-999). Air's strongest economic fit — teams large enough to feel the pain of disorganized assets, small enough to buy without a 6-month procurement cycle.
SMB (50-200) in a creative-heavy industry. Small enough to be led from the top, right-sized for a creative-ops tool's value prop.
Target industry. SaaS, MarTech, Creative Ops, Dev Tools, E-commerce, Media — industries with high creative-asset output where Air's value prop lands without translation.

Company runs Figma / Adobe / Canva / Frame.io. A detected creative stack is harder evidence of fit than an industry label — teams using these tools have creative volume to manage and recognize Air's category instantly.

C-Level or founder at a company under 50 employees. At this scale leadership is still the buyer AND the user — decision-to-close can happen in one call. Above 50 employees the buying motion specializes and a title boost starts double-counting.
Senior (C/VP/Director) in a marketing/creative function. Ideal Air buyer — budget authority AND the pain point is on their P&L.
Marketing/creative title without executive seniority. They feel the pain daily and often drive the bottom-up eval — less budget power, more product influence.
C-Level / VP / Director without a marketing title. Budget authority but not our target user — worth some weight, not a lot.
Active on 6+ channels. Multi-channel companies have bigger creative surface area — every channel is assets that need to be produced, reviewed, and distributed.

Enterprise (1000+) running on fewer than 3 channels. Contrarian signal: a large company with a narrow channel footprint is usually not under-ambitious, it's under-invested — there's whitespace a modern creative-ops tool could unlock. This is the opposite of a fit-and-activity rule; it specifically rewards *unrealized* potential.
8+ open creative roles. Strong hiring = scaling the creative org = rising budget for creative infrastructure.
3-7 open creative roles.

Enterprise + high activation. Rare and extremely high-confidence: a 1000+ employee account that has already uploaded 200+ assets and invited 5+ teammates is effectively pre-validated. Stacks on top of fit_enterprise intentionally — the point is that size *and* usage together is a much stronger signal than either alone.
High tech-modernity score (60+). Teams already running a modern creative+martech stack have the operational maturity to actually deploy another tool — lower implementation risk, faster time-to-value.
